nodes.label: Claude's review of the black-holes analysis
nodes.title: Claude's review of the LHC black-holes analysis

nodes.label: A-1 - A stated catastrophe probability is conditional on the argument's soundness and is dominated by argument-failure when very small
nodes.title: Any risk report's stated probability is P(X|A), the probability of catastrophe given its argument is sound; by the law of total probability P(X) = P(X|A)P(A) + P(X|¬A)P(¬A), so whenever the stated P(X|A) is far smaller than P(X|¬A)P(¬A), the actionable probability is set by the chance the argument is flawed - and a claim of strict impossibility (P(X|A)=0) is fully consistent with P(X) > 0.

nodes.label: A-11 - Thermality criteria require black-hole masses several times the Planck scale, out of reach of near-threshold LHC production
nodes.title: Every reasonable criterion for a produced object to be a genuinely thermal (semiclassical) black hole - Compton wavelength inside the Schwarzschild radius, small temperature change per emission, no single degree of freedom carrying a large energy fraction, lifetime exceeding both 1/M and the light-crossing time rS - requires the black-hole mass to sit several times (xmin ~ 3-16, and strictly ≫1) above the higher-dimensional Planck scale; combined with steeply falling parton luminosities, values of xmin that satisfy the criteria yield production rates too low for the LHC, so near-threshold objects dominate and are not trustworthy thermal black holes.

nodes.label: A-12 - Collisional inelasticity leaves only a fraction of parton energy behind the horizon, cutting black-hole cross sections by orders of magnitude
nodes.title: Classical trapped-surface calculations of ultrarelativistic collisions (D'eath-Payne; Eardley-Giddings; Yoshino et al.) show at most ~60% of the parton center-of-mass energy ends up inside the apparent horizon at zero impact parameter, and less at larger impact parameter (down to ~0 for 10D, ~0.2 for 5D at maximal b); folding this inelasticity into the impact-parameter-weighted cross section forces the PDFs to be probed at higher x and reduces the total black-hole production cross section by up to several orders of magnitude relative to the naive geometric estimate σ ≈ πrS².

nodes.label: A-13 - A measured 1.97-solar-mass neutron star bounds the maximum density of cold matter at about 4e15 g per cm3
nodes.title: Via EOS-independent analytic solutions of Einstein's equations, the existence of a 1.97 +/- 0.04 Msun neutron star sets an upper limit on the maximum possible mass density of stable cold matter of <~ 4e15 g cm^-3 (~10 times nuclear saturation density), and excludes every equation of state whose mass-radius curve never reaches 1.97 Msun.

nodes.label: A-14 - The measured E-half energy matches the parameter-insensitive GZK-proton prediction
nodes.title: The energy E_1/2 at which the integral spectrum falls to half its no-cutoff expectation is predicted to be 10^19.72 eV for protons under a wide range of assumptions about extragalactic sources (Berezinsky et al. 2006); TA measures log10(E_1/2/eV) = 19.72 +/- 0.05 (HiRes: 19.73 +/- 0.07), so the observed suppression sits quantitatively where the GZK proton mechanism - and not generic source physics - puts it.

nodes.label: A-15 - Kerr emission-rate calculation - spin-down precedes mass loss and rotation changes black-hole lifetime by at most a factor 2.7
nodes.title: Numerically integrating the Teukolsky-equation absorption probabilities (greybody factors, including superradiance) for massless spin 1/2, 1, and 2 fields on a Kerr background gives emission rates that rise steeply with specific angular momentum - power enhanced up to 13.35x (neutrinos), 107.5x (photons), 26,380x (gravitons) at extremality versus a Schwarzschild hole of equal mass - with angular momentum radiated several times faster than energy, so a fast-spinning hole spins down to near-zero rotation before losing most of its mass, and its total lifetime differs from the nonrotating case by only a factor 2.0-2.7.

nodes.label: A-16 - Declination-independence of the spectrum disfavors a few-source origin of the instep
nodes.title: A small number of discrete foreground sources would imprint their distinctive spectra anisotropically, making the measured energy spectrum vary between declination bands; the observed statistical identity of the spectra across -90 to +44.8 degrees (up to the known dipolar modulation) therefore disfavors attributing the instep feature to a few distinctive sources and favors a cause common to the whole sky.

nodes.label: A-17 - Steady spherical adiabatic accretion admits solutions only up to a maximal rate 4πλc(GM)²ρ∞ c∞^-3
nodes.title: For steady, spherically symmetric accretion of a polytropic gas (p ∝ ρ^γ, 1 ≤ γ ≤ 5/3) at rest at infinity onto a point mass M, the hydrodynamic equations admit solutions only for accretion rates Ṁ = 4πλ(GM)²ρ∞/c∞³ with λ ≤ λc(γ), where λc runs from e^{3/2}/4 ≈ 1.12 at γ=1 to 1/4 at γ=5/3 - the rate scales as M² ρ∞ c∞^-3 and is bounded above.

nodes.label: A-18 - Energy comparison selects the maximal-rate transonic solution as the state actually realized
nodes.title: Comparing the gas energy shell-by-shell across steady solutions shows the density (hence energy) in every spherical shell decreases as λ increases, and at λ = λc the transonic Type II state has lower density than Type I inside the sonic radius - so the maximal-rate transonic solution is the lowest-energy steady state and is the one expected to be physically realized.

nodes.label: A-2 - Independent sub-arguments multiplicatively shrink the probability that a safety case fails
nodes.title: The residual 'grey area' left by possible argument failure can never be removed entirely, but if a safety case contains multiple independent arguments A1, A2, ... then P(¬A1 ∧ ¬A2) < P(¬A1): total failure requires every layer to fail, so layered cases like Giddings-Mangano 2008 (rapid decay robust across theories; stable holes could not shed charge, making the cosmic-ray bound apply; astrophysical accretion limits) are substantially more reliable than any single argument.

nodes.label: A-22 - Orbital-decay agreement with GR excludes dipolar radiation and significant strong-field deviations for 2-solar-mass neutron stars
nodes.title: In consistent alternatives to GR, extra (e.g. scalar) fields generically produce dipolar gravitational radiation scaling as (alpha_PSR - alpha_WD)^2; because the white dwarf is weakly self-gravitating (alpha_WD ~ alpha_0, with |alpha_0| < 0.004 from Solar System tests), the measured Pb-dot(obs)/Pb-dot(GR) = 1.05 +/- 0.18 for PSR J0348+0432 implies |alpha_PSR - alpha_0| < 0.005 (95% CL), excluding significant strong-field scalarization even at ~2 Msun and limiting deviations of merger GW phase evolution from GR templates to < ~0.5 cycles for long-range fields.

nodes.label: A-23 - E-half agreement identifies the break as the GZK cutoff independently of the source spectral index
nodes.title: Because the theoretical E1/2 value (10^19.76 eV, Berezinsky-Grigor'eva) is robust across a wide range of source spectral slopes, the agreement of HiRes's measured E1/2 = 10^(19.73 ± 0.07) eV with it is strong evidence that the observed break is the proton-GZK cutoff rather than a source-spectrum artifact - a test insensitive to the injection power law, though it does not by itself exclude a heavy-composition (photodisintegration) origin of a suppression at a similar energy.

nodes.label: A-24 - Nature has already run the LHC collision programme 1e31 times on bodies that still exist, bounding any collider disaster mechanism
nodes.title: Since cosmic-ray collisions at centre-of-mass energies at or above the LHC's have occurred ~1e31 times on astronomical bodies whose continued, unperturbed existence we observe, any hypothetical disaster mechanism that LHC collisions could trigger would already have been triggered — so the stability of astronomical bodies constrains or excludes every such mechanism, up to one loophole: collider products are slow in the lab frame while cosmic-ray products are fast.

nodes.label: A-25 - Cosmic-ray collisions would already have nucleated any vacuum bubble the LHC could - and a bubble expands regardless of production frame
nodes.title: If LHC collisions could nucleate a bubble of lower-energy vacuum, cosmic-ray collisions of higher energy would have done so billions of years ago and the bubble would since have consumed much of the visible universe at light speed; the continued existence of the universe therefore excludes LHC-triggered vacuum decay — and the slow-vs-fast-products loophole does not apply, because a nucleated bubble propagates identically whatever the frame of its production.

nodes.label: A-26 - Dangerous magnetic monopoles are excluded - GUT monopoles are far too heavy for the LHC, and cosmic-ray-produced ones would have stopped in Earth harmlessly
nodes.title: Monopoles that catalyze nucleon decay are expected at masses ≥1e15 GeV, ~1e11 times beyond LHC reach; even granting a light proton-eating monopole, the 2003 study found it would destroy only about a microgram of matter before exiting the Earth, and — independently — any monopole the LHC could make, cosmic rays would already have made and (because its large quantized magnetic charge makes it heavily ionizing) stopped inside the Earth and other bodies, whose persistence shows no appreciable catalyzed proton decay occurs.

nodes.label: A-27 - Hawking radiation, resting on consensus quantum theory in curved space, makes LHC micro black holes evaporate
nodes.title: Quantum field theory in the curved space near any horizon forces particle-antiparticle pair creation, one partner escaping as radiation — so all black holes are unstable, microscopic ones evaporating essentially instantly; this reasoning holds equally in the extra-dimensional scenarios that motivate LHC black-hole production, though no experiment has yet directly detected Hawking radiation.

nodes.label: A-28 - Whatever colliding partons can singly produce can decay back into them - black hole stability would violate quantum mechanics
nodes.title: Independently of Hawking radiation, a black hole singly produced from colliding quarks and gluons carries no conserved quantum number the initial partons lack, so its decay back to partons is allowed, and in standard quantum physics the decay rate is directly tied to the production rate — a producible micro black hole cannot be completely black and must be short-lived; suppressing decay relative to production would violate quantum mechanics itself.

nodes.label: A-29 - Stable black holes from cosmic rays would be charged, stop in Earth or Sun, and accumulate in billions - the absence of any effect excludes the scenario
nodes.title: Black holes born from colliding quarks would mostly be electrically charged, and a charged stable black hole produced by cosmic rays would be slowed and stopped by well-understood electromagnetic interactions inside the Earth or Sun despite its high initial velocity; billions would have accumulated over their lifetimes if the LHC could produce them, so the complete absence of macroscopic effects means they are not produced, are all neutral, or are harmless — and neutrality plus stability is itself nearly self-contradictory, since a hole traversing matter preferentially absorbs nucleons and charges up positively while the Schwinger neutralization mechanism stands or falls with Hawking radiation.

nodes.label: A-3 - Weak-scale string tension is realizable only in type I string theory, with large extra dimensions from 0.1 fermi to 100 microns
nodes.title: Within perturbative string theory a ~TeV string scale cannot be realized in the heterotic theory but is consistently realized in type I theory - and then necessarily comes with n = 2-6 extra dimensions of radius ~0.1 fermi up to ~100 microns and an O(1) string coupling - so the ADD TeV-gravity premise has a concrete, calculable string-theoretic embedding rather than being field-theoretically ad hoc.

nodes.label: A-30 - Even hypothetically stable neutral black holes would have destroyed observed white dwarfs and neutron stars - their survival closes the last case
nodes.title: Granting stable, neutral, accreting micro black holes for the sake of argument, the Giddings-Mangano analysis bounds their growth with conservative macroscopic-physics accretion scenarios: with seven or more spacetime dimensions at the LHC scale, accretion in Earth is so slow that harm would take billions of years; with five or six dimensions, accretion is fast — but then cosmic-ray-produced holes, stopped even when neutral inside dense white dwarfs and neutron stars, would have destroyed those stars in highly visible events on timescales far shorter than their observed lifetimes; the observed persistence of white dwarfs and neutron stars therefore shows such holes are not produced, at the LHC or anywhere.

nodes.label: A-32 - Lunar survival under billions of years of heavy-ion cosmic-ray collisions rules out dangerous strangelet production - RHIC data validate both assumptions
nodes.title: Cosmic-ray iron nuclei have collided with iron in the unshielded lunar surface for billions of years at energies comparable to or above collider heavy-ion experiments, and the Moon persists — excluding dangerous strangelet production in such collisions; the argument's two assumptions (iron-iron collisions reach conditions comparable to gold/lead collisions, and produced strangelets are not all too fast to stop and coalesce) are now supported by RHIC data: Cu+Cu collisions follow the same thermal systematics as Au+Au, and measured velocity distributions are as broad as or broader than assumed.

nodes.label: A-33 - Interstellar heavy-ion collisions would have seeded stars with strangelets - the absence of strangelet-triggered stellar explosions bounds the danger with no velocity assumption
nodes.title: The known rate of cosmic-ray heavy-ion collisions in interstellar space implies that, were dangerous strangelets produced, some would be swept into forming stars, whose large-scale conversion would appear as anomalous stellar explosions; none are seen, providing a safety bound independent of any assumption about strangelet production velocities — its one assumption being that produced strangelets do not decay on timescales much shorter than star formation.

nodes.label: A-34 - Replacing the collapse by past-horizon boundary conditions reproduces the Hawking flux
nodes.title: The gravitational collapse in Hawking's derivation can be replaced by boundary conditions on the past horizon of the analytically extended black-hole spacetime while retaining the essential features of the collapse calculation; the same thermal flux results, showing the evaporation prediction does not depend on the details of the collapse, and the same boundary conditions are the natural choice for a preexistent (eternal) black hole.

nodes.label: A-35 - Accelerated detectors respond thermally in vacuum while geodesic detectors near the horizon see no Hawking flux
nodes.title: A uniformly accelerated particle detector in flat-spacetime vacuum registers a thermal particle flux (the Unruh effect), behaving like a detector held static near a black-hole horizon; conversely a geodesic (freely falling) detector near the horizon sees no Hawking flux - so 'particle' content is observer-dependent, which both supports interpreting the Hawking flux as what distant/static observers genuinely measure and delimits what the flux physically is.

nodes.label: A-36 - In a 2D model the computed Hawking outflow depends on the regularization - covariant fermion-boson cancellation gives zero
nodes.title: In a two-dimensional black-hole model, applying fermion-boson cancellation on the stress-energy tensor reduces the computed energy outflow to zero, while other, noncovariant regularization techniques reproduce the Hawking result - demonstrating that the derived evaporation flux can depend on the choice of regularization scheme, a caveat on the robustness of the semiclassical derivation.

nodes.label: A-37 - Outgoing Hawking modes trace back to trans-Planckian frequencies at the horizon
nodes.title: In the standard derivation, an outgoing Hawking quantum observed at late times traces back, through the exponential red-shift at the horizon, to field modes of formally unbounded (trans-Planckian) frequency near the horizon - so the derivation formally invokes physics at scales where quantum field theory in curved spacetime cannot be trusted, the technical origin of the 'trans-Planckian problem'.

nodes.label: A-38 - QFT on a collapsing-star background yields a thermal emission spectrum independent of collapse details
nodes.title: Quantizing a free field on the classical spacetime of a star collapsing to a black hole, the late-time Bogoliubov mixing of positive and negative frequencies is fixed solely by the horizon's surface gravity, giving an outgoing particle flux exactly thermal at T = kappa/2pi in every mode — independent of the details of the collapse and holding for bosons, fermions, higher spins, massive fields, and rotating or charged holes.

nodes.label: A-39 - A negative energy flux across the horizon forces mass loss and a finite lifetime of order 1e-28 M cubed seconds
nodes.title: Any renormalized stress tensor that is conserved, stationary at late times, and agrees with the normal-ordered operator near future null infinity gives the same positive outward energy flux through every constant-r surface, hence a negative energy flux into the hole: the horizon area must shrink, the mass decreases at the thermal rate, and the hole disappears in of order 10^-28 M^3 s (M in grams) — so primordial holes below ~10^15 g are gone by today, and the quasi-stationary description holds until the mass nears the Planck mass 10^-5 g, when little energy remains.

nodes.label: A-4 - The warped two-brane metric solves Einstein's equations and yields the exponential hierarchy from krc of about 50
nodes.title: An exact solution of the 5D Einstein equations with two 3-branes of opposite tension bounding a slice of AdS5 exists, in which the 4D Planck mass is essentially independent of the compactification radius while every visible-brane mass parameter is rescaled by e^(-k rc pi) - so krc ~ 11-12, i.e. k rc pi ~ 35 (an O(10) input, no large number; the paper's own 'krc ~ 50' phrasing is consistent only as M rc ~ 50 with k a factor of a few below M) generates the full 10^15 weak-Planck hierarchy.

nodes.label: A-40 - Black-hole thermodynamics independently demands emission at exactly kappa over 2 pi
nodes.title: The classical laws of black-hole mechanics (dM = kappa/8pi dA + Omega dJ; area never decreases; kappa constant on the horizon) mirror the laws of thermodynamics with kappa as temperature and A as entropy; a black hole that only absorbed would violate the Generalized Second Law when immersed in colder black-body radiation — steady emission at precisely T = kappa/2pi is what makes the identification consistent, which is strong independent support that the derived rate is right.

nodes.label: A-41 - Greybody-corrected numerical calculation fixes the evaporation power and its division among species
nodes.title: Combining Hawking's thermal formalism with Teukolsky-Press black-hole perturbation methods to compute the frequency- and spin-dependent transmission (greybody) factors, the total emission power of an uncharged nonrotating hole of mass M >> 10^17 g is 2 x 10^-4 hbar c^6 G^-2 M^-2, distributed 81% neutrinos, 17% photons, 2% gravitons; extending with estimated massive-particle emission for smaller (hotter) holes converts this into the M^3 lifetime law and the (5 +/- 1) x 10^14 g decayed-by-now threshold.

nodes.label: A-42 - Solar capture and catalysis kinematics convert the Super-K neutrino limit into a monopole-flux bound
nodes.title: Modeling the Sun as a gravitational monopole collector over its 4.6 Gyr life, the catalyzed proton-decay rate implied by a given ambient monopole flux scales the expected solar low-energy neutrino flux; inverting this chain, Super-K's I90 = 166.6 cm^-2 s^-1 limit becomes N_M (sigma_0/1mb)(f_pi+/0.5) < 7.1 x 10^17 captured monopoles and hence F_M (sigma_0/1mb) < 6.3 x 10^-24 (beta_M/10^-3)^2 cm^-2 s^-1 sr^-1 (90% CL, beta_M < 10^-3, M_M up to ~10^17 GeV).

nodes.label: A-43 - The 10^18.5 eV dip survives resolution, aperture, and epoch checks, so it is a real spectral feature
nodes.title: The dip cannot be an artifact of energy resolution (the resolution is approximately constant across the dip region), of aperture modeling (the dip is visible in the raw E^1.5-weighted event distribution before any aperture correction), or of detector evolution (it appears at the same energy in the best-resolution epochs analyzed separately); conversely, the smoother monocular spectrum is fully reproduced by smearing the stereo spectrum with the monocular resolution, explaining the mono-stereo difference.

nodes.label: A-44 - Basic quantum principles imply LHC-scale black holes decay in about 1e-27 s - stability would need physics with no consistent framework
nodes.title: A black hole producible in LHC parton collisions carries no conserved charge not carried by ordinary matter, so basic quantum mechanics dictates it decays to ordinary matter; the only dimensionful scale available is t_D ~ 1/M_D ~ 10^-27 s with no small parameter to suppress the decay, and every framework in which the Schwinger discharge mechanism operates also produces Hawking decay — making the stable-but-neutralizing scenario nearly self-contradictory.

nodes.label: A-45 - Charged stable black holes would stop in Earth or the Sun, so their multi-billion-year survival excludes that scenario
nodes.title: A cosmic-ray-produced black hole retaining electric charge loses energy by standard electromagnetic processes exactly like a heavy muon: Earth's column density stops singly-charged holes up to ~7 TeV and the Sun's core stops masses well beyond 100 TeV — so if stable charged black holes could be made at LHC energies, cosmic rays would have been implanting them in Earth and Sun for billions of years, and the continued existence of both rules out any risk from charged stable black holes.

nodes.label: A-46 - Neutral cosmic-ray-produced black holes punch through Earth and Sun, voiding the naive cosmic-ray safety argument
nodes.title: A neutral black hole interacts only gravitationally, and the column density needed to stop one — set by d_0 = M_0^3/(pi rho) ~ 3 x 10^11 cm at Earth density — exceeds what Earth, Sun, planets, or ordinary stars provide; cosmic-ray-produced holes (born with gamma up to ~4.5 x 10^4) therefore traverse and escape these bodies, so Earth's 4.5-Gyr survival does NOT by itself bound stable neutral black holes; only bodies with d_0 comparable to their size — white dwarfs (~1.5 km at 10^7 g/cm^3) and neutron stars (<~0.01 cm) — can serve as beam dumps.

nodes.label: A-47 - For crossover radius below about 200 Angstrom a stable hole in Earth accretes far slower than the solar lifetime
nodes.title: First-principles accretion of a trapped stable hole inside Earth — electromagnetic-competition capture at subatomic scales, Bondi hydrodynamic capture at macroscopic scales, evaluated with uniformly growth-maximizing assumptions — gives timescales of ~3 x 10^11 yr (D >= 8 subatomic 4D phase) and ~1.2 x 10^12/lambda_4 yr (4D Bondi doubling from ~10^11 g), and 6.4-94 Gyr total for D = 7: whenever gravity turns four-dimensional below R_C ~ 200 Angstrom, Earth accretion is irrelevant on the Sun's ~5 Gyr remaining lifetime, with the hole still under ~10^11 g (power ~300 kW) at solar-lifetime times.

nodes.label: A-48 - For crossover radius above about 200 Angstrom Earth accretion could be fast, so Earth-internal calculation alone cannot close the safety case
nodes.title: When the D-dimensional force law extends into the macroscopic regime — unwarped D = 6 (R_D ~ 5 x 10^-2 cm), D = 5 warped with R_C near the experimental bound (~0.2 mm), and generally R_C >> 200 Angstrom — the same conservative accretion formalism yields Earth-consumption bounds as short as ~10^4-10^5 yr (D = 6: ~9.7 x 10^4 (M_D/M_0)^2/lambda_6,4 yr; warped D = 5: ~3 x 10^5 yr), so these scenarios are genuinely dangerous if stable holes exist and are trapped — the safety conclusion for them must come from compact-star bounds, not from Earth.

nodes.label: A-49 - Solar-mass white dwarfs provide enough column density to stop cosmic-ray-produced neutral black holes for D up to 7
nodes.title: Integrating gravitational accretion and scattering slow-down through white-dwarf matter, the column density required to stop a neutral black hole of 7-14 TeV is 0.09-0.70 x 10^15 g/cm^2 (D=5), 0.65-6.5 (D=6), 1.8-20.2 (D=7), versus available diameters of 13, 21, 38 x 10^15 g/cm^2 for 1.0, 1.1, 1.2 solar-mass white dwarfs (+/-10% systematic): D = 5, 6 holes stop in a solar-mass star, D = 7 needs >~ 1.1 solar masses, and D >= 8 (3.3-40 x 10^15) generally exceeds white-dwarf stopping power, which is why D >= 8 is bounded with neutron stars instead.

nodes.label: A-5 - Absence of light KK modes exempts RS1 from the astrophysical and cosmological bounds on large extra dimensions
nodes.title: Because the RS1 Kaluza-Klein excitation scale is ~TeV rather than sub-eV, none of the particle-physics, astrophysical, or cosmological constraints on large-extra-dimension (ADD-type) scenarios apply to RS1 - so no experimental bound pushes its effective strong-gravity scale far above a TeV.

nodes.label: A-50 - Cosmic rays produce thousands of black holes on low-field white dwarfs within tens of millions of years under any composition
nodes.title: Convolving the measured Auger flux with CTEQ6M parton densities and conservative trapped-surface inelasticity, a 5400-km white dwarf accumulates 2 x 10^6 - 6.7 x 10^7 black holes per Myr for proton primaries and still 7 x 10^3 - 2.6 x 10^5 per Myr for 100% iron (D = 5-7, M_min = 7-14 TeV); magnetic screening is negligible for B_p <~ few x 10^5 G, and even the extreme worst case (pure Fe, y = 0.5, 14 TeV, stopping-geometry acceptance) leaves > 100 accumulated holes within 50 Myr — production is never the bottleneck of the white-dwarf bound.

nodes.label: A-51 - White-dwarf survival excludes every scenario in which Earth accretion would be dangerous - the accretion-time ratio is about 2e4
nodes.title: A stable black hole trapped in a white dwarf accretes ~1.9 x 10^4 times faster than in Earth (same 4D Bondi physics, denser liquid medium: d_0 c_s smaller by ~1.5 x 10^-4): unwarped D = 6 phases total tens to hundreds of years, D = 7 at most ~80 Myr, and any warped scenario with R_C >~ 15 Angstrom finishes within ~5 x 10^6 yr — so white dwarfs observed to be >~ Gyr old could not exist if such holes were stable and produced; combined with the slow-Earth-accretion result for R_C <~ 200 Angstrom this closes the safety dichotomy: every configuration that could harm Earth within the solar lifetime is empirically excluded.

nodes.label: A-52 - Neutron-star survival bounds the D of 8 and higher scenarios via binary beam dumps and cosmogenic neutrinos
nodes.title: Neutron stars stop any black hole (d_0 <~ 0.01 cm), and although their >~ 10^8 G fields synchrotron-screen direct charged cosmic rays (acceptance ~10^-3), two production routes survive: binary companions acting as unmagnetized beam dumps (2-30 Myr full-coverage-equivalent; 54-633 holes per FCE-Myr for D = 8-11 at M_min = 14-7 TeV) and GZK cosmogenic neutrinos (>~ 4.5 x 10^3 - 6 x 10^4 per Myr even with a worst-case flux); a captured hole penetrates the crust in <~ 10 s x r_c[1/TeV] and consumes the star within ~10 Myr (D = 11 worst case; <~ 50 yr for D <= 7) — so Gyr-old neutron stars and X-ray binaries exclude stable-black-hole scenarios for D >= 8 unless cosmic rays are almost purely heavy AND the cosmogenic neutrino flux is absent or non-reactive.

nodes.label: A-53 - No Eddington limit throttles micro-black-hole accretion in Earth, white dwarfs, or neutron stars
nodes.title: Radiation emitted by matter falling into a small black hole cannot build the outward pressure needed to stall accretion: spherical accretion is radiatively inefficient, photon pressure is many orders below fluid pressure (p_gamma/p ~ 10^-6 in white dwarfs, ~4 x 10^-12 in Earth, <~ 10^-11 in neutron stars), and in dense media the photons are trapped and advected inward — so Bondi rates stand; independently, if Eddington-limited emission did operate in white dwarfs, the accumulated holes' luminosity (exceeding 10^-2 solar for N R_B >~ 60 cm) would visibly disrupt observed white-dwarf cooling.

nodes.label: A-54 - Conservative overestimates give k_cl = 1e-22 and k_qu = 1e-34 for RHIC - gravitational collapse is absurdly out of reach
nodes.title: Assuming all collision energy (M = 1e4 GeV) concentrates in the Lorentz-contracted nuclear volume (R = 1e-15 cm) — a wild overestimate that also ignores charge and momentum resisting collapse — the black-hole criticality parameter k_cl = 2GM/Rc2 is ~3e-33 (the source prints 1e-22, an ~11-order arithmetic overstatement in the conservative direction), and the graviton-emission parameter k_qu = GE2/(hbar c5) at E ≈ 200 GeV is 1e-34; both so far below unity that refinement is pointless.

nodes.label: A-55 - 1e47 comparable cosmic-ray collisions in our past light cone bound a RHIC-triggered vacuum transition below 2e-36
nodes.title: If heavy-ion collisions at RHIC energies could trigger vacuum decay with probability p-bar per collision, the ~1e47 iron-iron collisions above 100 GeV/nucleon in our past light cone would have triggered it long ago unless p-bar <~ 1e-47; treating past-light-cone survival as an unbiased observation — i.e. setting aside observer-selection effects, which a decay-destroys-all-observers catastrophe maximally invokes — our existence bounds the probability of a vacuum transition during RHIC's ~2e11-collision lifetime below 2e-36 (iron), or 2e-26 conservatively demanding Z > 70 projectiles at abundance 1e-5; selection-aware reformulations (e.g. from the lateness of our evolution) retain a bound, but a far weaker one.

nodes.label: A-56 - A strangelet catastrophe requires four independent conditions, each judged unlikely - with no plausible route to the last two
nodes.title: Dangerous strangelet production at a heavy-ion collider requires the conjunction of four independent conditions: (1) bulk strange matter absolutely stable at zero pressure; (2) strangelets at least metastable at the very small A a collision can make — but surface energy, a robust feature of degenerate fermion systems, destabilizes small strangelets (A below ~10-30 disfavored; for 2 < A ≤ 6 QCD strongly suggests complete instability, the best candidate being the uuddss H-dibaryon, empirically no lighter than the deuteron); (3) producibility in the collision environment; (4) negative charge along the stability line, though Fermi-gas kinematics gives strange matter positive charge; the authors know of no plausible suggestion for why (3) or especially (4) might hold, and failure of any one condition kills the scenario.

nodes.label: A-57 - Stable strange matter is overwhelmingly likely positively charged - and a positive strangelet is harmless, its fusion Coulomb-suppressed to a rate of 1e-200000 per second
nodes.title: Because s quarks are heavier, Fermi-gas kinematics leaves strange matter positively charged per unit baryon number; making the charge negative requires gluon-exchange effects so strong they simultaneously unbind the matter unless the bag constant takes unreasonably low values — so negative stable strange matter is extremely unlikely; and a positively charged strangelet is no hazard at all: it captures electrons into an exotic atom whose nucleus is shielded from fusion exactly as ordinary nuclei are, a room-temperature A=396, Z=6 strangelet fusing with hydrogen at the calculated rate of ~1e-200000 per second.

nodes.label: A-58 - Coalescence and thermal production models bound dangerous strangelet yield near 1e-46 per collision, peaking below AGS energies rather than at RHIC
nodes.title: Both empirically calibrated production mechanisms make a dangerous strangelet hopeless at RHIC: the AGS-fitted coalescence model (penalty 0.02 per added baryon, 0.2 per unit strangeness, transition to a single quark bag generously taken at unit probability) yields ~1e-46 per collision for an A=20, Z=-1, S=22 strangelet, i.e. p ≈ 2e-35 over RHIC's lifetime; the thermal model (T = 140 MeV, mu_B = 540 MeV at AGS) gives ~2e-27 per central collision even for a benign A=20, Z=+2, S=16 strangelet, with negative-charge yields far smaller — and both models predict production falls as collision energy rises, so the optimum energy lies below the AGS, while the speculative strangeness-distillation mechanism is contradicted by the observed rapid adiabatic expansion and the failure of CERN heavy-ion experiments to see strangelets despite probable QGP formation.

nodes.label: A-59 - Lunar persistence bounds dangerous strangelet production at p below 2e-11 - unless production is confined to exactly central rapidity
nodes.title: Folding the ~1e28 lunar Fe-Fe collisions at AGS-equivalent energies with a strangelet stopping-survival model (geometric dissociation cross-section, ionization stopping) gives an effective exposure of ~1e22 x p even with a punishing 1e-6 rapidity-suppression factor, so the Moon's survival bounds the per-collision dangerous-strangelet probability at p < 2e-11 for the physically favored case (Fe-Fe, AGS energies, power-law rapidity distribution) — a bound destroyed only by assuming production occurs solely in gold-gold collisions, only at RHIC energies and above, and only exactly at rest in the CM, assumptions the authors consider physically unmotivated.

nodes.label: A-6 - The vacuum-stability verdict hinges on the top-quark mass input and on assuming no new physics below the Planck scale
nodes.title: The metastability conclusion is a ~2-sigma verdict conditional on two hinges - the top-quark pole mass (the dominant uncertainty - 1 sigma in Mt moves the critical Mh by 1.4 GeV, and Mt below ~171 GeV would restore lambda(M_Pl) >= 0) and the assumption that no new physics or unknown Planck-scale threshold corrections modify the SM potential below M_Pl.

nodes.label: A-60 - Strangelets born in interstellar space would convert stars in supernova-like events - the observed supernova rate bounds RHIC danger below 2e-8, tightening to 1e-21 - unless strangelets are metastable
nodes.title: Dangerous strangelets produced at rest by cosmic-ray collisions in interstellar space would be swept into forming stars (~1e57 cm3 of material per star) and trigger their conversion — supernova-like events; the observed rate of a few supernovae per millennium per galaxy bounds the probability of dangerous strangelet production over RHIC's lifetime below 2e-8 even under Dar-De Rujula-Heinz's restrictive worst-case assumptions (gold on gold, RHIC energies, central rapidity only), and below ~1e-21 with the better physics of AGS-optimal energies (factor 4e4) and iron projectiles (factor 1e10); the bound's soft spot is metastability — a strangelet decaying by baryon emission with lifetime between ~1e-7 s and the Myr of star formation evades it while remaining dangerous at RHIC.

nodes.label: A-61 - Gauss law in 4+n dimensions ties a TeV fundamental scale to R of 1e30overn minus 17 cm - excluding n=1 and hiding n of 2 or more from all existing tests
nodes.title: Gauss's law in 4+n dimensions gives V(r) ∝ 1/(M_(4+n)^(n+2) r^(n+1)) for r << R and the usual 1/r potential with M_Pl^2 ~ M_(4+n)^(2+n) R^n for r >> R; demanding M_(4+n) ~ 1 TeV and the observed M_Pl fixes R ~ 1e(30/n - 17) cm — so n=1 (R ~ 1e13 cm, solar-system scales) is empirically excluded, while for every n ≥ 2 the modification of gravity sets in below the distances probed by any 1998 experiment (n=2: R ~ 100 microns - 1 mm, exactly the range planned sub-millimeter gravity experiments would reach).

nodes.label: A-62 - Graviton emission scales as ΔE to the n+2 over m_EW - so 1998 lab and astrophysical data fail to exclude TeV-scale gravity
nodes.title: Each Kaluza-Klein graviton couples with ordinary 1/M_Pl strength, and only the multiplicity (ΔE R)^n compensates, so the branching ratio for graviton emission in any process is ~(ΔE/m_EW)^(n+2); evaluating this against every relevant 1998 dataset — Upsilon decays (~1e-8), Z decays (~1e-5), solar cooling (effective F ~ 1e12 GeV vs 1e7 probed), red giants (1e10 vs 1e9), SN 1987A (1e8 vs the 1e10 claimed limit), and ultra-high-energy cosmic rays (only soft QCD interactions, no hard collisions) — leaves the framework unexcluded, the single serious tension (SN 1987A at n=2) resolvable by m_EW ≳ 10 TeV or n > 2, and all astrophysical bounds lapsing for n ≳ 7.

nodes.label: A-63 - A nucleated true-vacuum bubble has zero total energy and expands at light speed, destroying the causal region with no warning
nodes.title: Once a true-vacuum bubble materializes, energy conservation makes its total energy exactly zero (negative interior canceling positive wall), so by spherical symmetry the gravitational field at the wall's outer edge vanishes and nothing retards its growth: the wall accelerates asymptotically to the speed of light, arrives essentially without warning, converts the false vacuum, and inside it the constants of nature differ — life and chemistry as we know them become impossible — while for decay into a negative-energy vacuum the anti-de-Sitter interior itself suffers gravitational collapse within microseconds.

nodes.label: A-64 - Gravitational back-reaction suppresses decay to a negative-energy vacuum and quenches it entirely when epsilon is at or below 6 pi G sigma-squared
nodes.title: Including gravitation in the bounce calculation (thin-wall limit): for decay from a zero-cosmological-constant false vacuum to a true vacuum of energy density -epsilon, gravity raises the bounce action and enlarges the critical bubble, and when epsilon ≤ (3/4) kappa sigma^2 = 6 pi G sigma^2 (equivalently rho_0 = 2 Lambda, eq. 3.21) the bubble radius diverges and the decay probability vanishes — gravitation totally quenches vacuum decay and stabilizes the false vacuum; in the opposite case (decay from a positive-energy vacuum into the present one), gravity works the other way, shrinking the bubble and making decay more likely.

nodes.label: A-65 - If stable strange matter exists, nuclearites arrive at Earth as detectable cosmic radiation - nature is already running the exposure experiment
nodes.title: If bulk strange quark matter is stable over the full range of baryon numbers, then macroscopic strange-matter nuggets should exist in the Galaxy and arrive as a component of cosmic radiation colliding with Earth ('nuclearites'), producing characteristic, experimentally detectable encounter signatures — so the existence of stable strange matter is empirically testable from Earth, and Earth's own long history of such encounters without incident is itself an exposure record: stable strange matter raining on Earth does not convert or destroy it.

nodes.label: A-7 - Eddington-limited radiating micro black holes would be undetectable in white dwarfs and neutron stars, evading the astrophysical exclusion
nodes.title: A metastable micro black hole accreting at the Eddington limit inside a white dwarf or neutron star would be observationally invisible and would consume the star far more slowly than its age - so the Giddings-Mangano survival argument from observed old white dwarfs and neutron stars does not exclude Earth-dangerous Eddington-limited micro black holes.

nodes.label: A-8 - The white-dwarf stopping argument presumes semiclassical validity and fails for black holes below three times the Planck mass scale
nodes.title: Giddings-Mangano's demonstration that cosmic-ray-produced micro black holes are stopped inside white dwarfs assumes the semiclassical description, which they themselves restrict to masses M > M_min = 3 M_5; black holes produced below M_min, deep in the quantum-gravity regime, might have smaller stopping cross sections and escape white dwarfs as they escape ordinary stars - a gap that would void the astrophysical exclusion of dangerous black holes independently of the metastability scenario.

nodes.label: A-9 - The allowed warping-length range up to 44 microns permits Casadio-Harms critical masses for which black-hole growth is catastrophic
nodes.title: Catastrophic growth in the Casadio-Harms framework requires the critical mass M_c to exceed ~1.3x10^4 kg; Casadio et al.'s own defining conditions give M_c above that threshold for all warping lengths L > ~15 microns, and the experimental limit allows L up to 44 microns (M_c up to 1.1x10^5 - 2.2x10^6 kg, or 5.9x10^22 kg under their alternative eq. 16) - so their benign verdict rests on an unexplained restriction to L ~ 1 micron, not on evidence.

nodes.label: A-10 - The large monopole-photon coupling makes leading-order cross sections unreliable, so the MoEDAL mass limits are indicative only
nodes.title: Because the Dirac charge g_D ~ 68.5e puts the monopole-photon coupling far outside the perturbative regime, the tree-level Drell-Yan and photon-fusion cross sections used to convert MoEDAL's model-independent cross-section limits into mass exclusions are not trustworthy - the 1500-3750 GeV mass limits are indicative, not firm exclusions of TeV-scale monopoles.

nodes.label: A-19 - Anthropic-shadow correction - observed catastrophe frequencies underestimate true rates, without bound as survival probability goes to zero
nodes.title: Conditioning on the existence of present observers biases historically inferred catastrophe frequencies downward - Bayes' formula gives posterior P(catastrophe occurred | we exist) = PQ/(1 - P + PQ) for prior probability P and survival probability Q, so the overconfidence factor eta = (1 - P + PQ)/Q grows beyond 1 as events become more destructive and diverges as Q -> 0; frequency estimates for near-certainly-fatal event classes can understate the true rate by orders of magnitude.

nodes.label: A-20 - Cosmic-ray and solar-system-survival safety arguments are unreliable for observer-extinguishing event classes
nodes.title: For an event class with survival probability Q = 0 (vacuum phase transition; any Earth-sterilizing collider disaster), probability estimates based on the observation that Earth (and the Moon) have survived ~4.5 Gyr of cosmic-ray bombardment - the Hut-Rees argument - or on the solar system's duration so far - invoked in the LHC Safety Assessment Group study - are completely unreliable, because our existence is a precondition for making the observation regardless of the true rate.

nodes.label: A-21 - Evidence not conditioned on terrestrial survival - astronomical observations - largely escapes the anthropic shadow
nodes.title: The anthropic-shadow bias applies only to evidence whose existence is contingent on observers' survival; risk estimates grounded in astronomical knowledge not derived from terrestrial history - the census of the solar neighborhood, the mass function of stellar objects, observations of distant galaxies - are "not appreciably afflicted by anthropic bias" and can render conclusions about such risks bias-free.

nodes.label: A-31 - Producing a cold bound strangelet in a 1.6-trillion-degree fireball is thermodynamically suppressed below one nucleus per thousand universe-lifetimes of LHC running
nodes.title: A strangelet is bound by O(1-10) MeV while heavy-ion collisions make heat baths of T ≈ 165 MeV, so condensation is fought by the second law — an ice cube in a furnace; quantitatively, the measured thermal penalty factor exp(-(m_N - mu_B)/T) suppresses production of even a normal A=10 nucleus by ~3e-25 per nucleon rate (A=20: ~1e-49), giving probability ~1e-13 over the LHC's ~1e10 relevant heavy-ion collisions, with strangelets suppressed further still; and because the LHC runs at least as hot as RHIC with lower net baryon density, strangelet production is less likely at the LHC than at RHIC, which was less likely than the 1980s-90s machines.

nodes.label: H-1 - Neutron-star matter has a stiff equation of state - no hyperons, bosons, or free quarks near nuclear saturation density
nodes.title: The equation of state of cold matter at supranuclear densities is stiff enough to support neutron stars of at least 1.97 Msun; EOS models that soften through the appearance of exotic hadronic matter (hyperons, kaon condensates) or free quarks at a few times nuclear saturation density are not realized in neutron-star interiors (condensed quark matter is not fully excluded but is strongly constrained).

nodes.label: H-10 - Rotating micro black holes evaporate essentially as fast as nonrotating ones - lifetime differs by at most a factor 2.7
nodes.title: Black holes emit a physically real thermal Hawking flux at temperature kappa/2pi, independent of the details of their formation; the emission drains the hole's mass with lifetime scaling as M^3 (a primordial hole has fully decayed within the age of the universe iff its initial mass was below about 5e14 g), rotation changing the total lifetime by at most a factor ~2.7; hence any microscopic black hole produced at the LHC decays essentially instantly (~1e-27 s) rather than persisting.

nodes.label: H-11 - The actionable LHC catastrophe probability is dominated by the chance the safety argument itself is flawed
nodes.title: For the LHC safety case, the probability that the argument itself is flawed is plausibly at least 1e-4 and more likely above 1e-3 (based on the historical error rate of expert arguments), so the all-things-considered probability of catastrophe P(X) is dominated by the argument-failure term P(X|¬A)P(¬A) and sits orders of magnitude above stated conditional estimates such as 1e-9 - i.e. the safety conclusion hinges on the soundness of the argument, which internal rigor alone cannot certify.

nodes.label: H-12 - The instep and steepening reflect overlapping helium and CNO flux components with a narrow source rigidity maximum, not a few foreground sources
nodes.title: The instep feature (~13 EeV) and the steepening arise from the interplay of helium and carbon-nitrogen-oxygen mass components emitted by a source population with a narrow range of maximum rigidity - the succession of rather pure nuclear composition groups above 10 EeV - rather than from the distinctive spectrum of a small number of foreground sources contributing significantly to the total intensity.

nodes.label: H-13 - General relativity correctly describes gravity and gravitational radiation even in the strong-field regime of a 2-solar-mass neutron star
nodes.title: General relativity remains an accurate description of gravity - with no significant dipolar gravitational radiation or strong-field scalarization phenomena from long-range extra fields - even at the extreme gravitational binding energies of a ~2-Msun neutron star; short-range deviations (extra fields with mass heavier than ~1e-19 eV/c^2) are not excluded by this system.

nodes.label: H-14 - LHC strong-gravity objects would be near-threshold quantum or stringy states, not semiclassical thermal black holes
nodes.title: In low-scale-gravity scenarios, objects produced at the LHC would almost always be near-threshold, low-entropy quantum-gravitational or stringy states below the semiclassical regime; truly thermal black holes (the regime where the standard Hawking-evaporation description applies) are very unlikely to be produced at all, and if so only in a very limited parameter regime.

nodes.label: H-15 - The first observable low-scale quantum-gravity signature at the LHC would be enhanced transverse two-body final states
nodes.title: If the higher-dimensional quantum-gravity scale is low, the LHC's earliest and strongest quantum-gravity signal would be an enhancement of highly transverse two-particle final states (dijet and, over a smaller parameter range, leptonic) near the quantum-gravity scale - detectable by compositeness-type searches with reach of order 5 TeV - rather than the widely advertised multiparticle thermal-black-hole signature.

nodes.label: H-16 - Physical accretion realizes the maximal transonic rate, with an interpolation formula covering the moving-star case
nodes.title: In steady spherical accretion the physically realized state is the maximal-rate transonic (Type II) solution, λ = λc(γ); and the interpolation Ṁ ≈ 2π(GM)²(V² + c²)^(-3/2) ρ∞ gives the order of magnitude of the accretion rate for the general case of a mass M moving at velocity V through gas of ambient density ρ∞ and sound speed c.

nodes.label: H-2 - TeV-scale type-I string theory with large extra dimensions solves the hierarchy problem
nodes.title: The fundamental scale of gravity lies near 1 TeV: Standard Model fields are confined to a (3+1)-dimensional brane while gravity propagates in n >= 2 large extra dimensions (from ~0.1 fermi up to ~1 mm), the apparent 4D Planck scale being the volume dilution of TeV-scale gravity; realized either purely field-theoretically (ADD) or as TeV-scale type-I string theory with SM fields as open strings on a D3-brane, this eliminates the weak-Planck hierarchy without low-energy supersymmetry or technicolor.

nodes.label: H-20 - Solar-captured GUT monopoles catalyze proton decay producing a detectable low-energy neutrino flux
nodes.title: A cosmic flux of supermassive GUT monopoles (at or below the Parker bound ~10^-15 cm^-2 s^-1 sr^-1) exists; monopoles gravitationally captured by the Sun accumulate at its center and catalyze proton decay with strong-interaction-scale cross sections via the Callan-Rubakov process, ultimately yielding nu_e, nu_mu and anti-nu_mu (from pi+ -> mu+ -> e+ decay chains) detectable at Earth as a ~20-55 MeV neutrino flux.

nodes.label: H-21 - The dip near 10^18.5 eV marks a transition from heavy Galactic to light extragalactic cosmic rays
nodes.title: The spectral dip near 10^18.5 eV reflects a change of cosmic-ray origin with energy: below the break the flux is mostly Galactic and heavy (iron-dominated, confined by the Galactic magnetic field), above it an extragalactic, predominantly protonic component takes over; a two-power-law superposition fit gives iron/proton flux ratio = 10^(-0.887 x [log E - 18.50]) and predicts an X_max elongation rate of 81 g/cm^2 per decade between 10^18 and 10^19 eV, close to the observed 78.9 +/- 3 g/cm^2.

nodes.label: H-22 - Even hypothetically stable LHC black holes pose no risk to Earth on timescales below the Earth's natural lifetime
nodes.title: Even under the extreme hypothesis that a TeV-scale black hole produced at the LHC is absolutely stable and trapped inside Earth, its accretion causes no macroscopic effect on any timescale short compared with the Sun's natural ~5-billion-year remaining lifetime; every scenario in which accretion could be faster is excluded by the survival of observed white dwarfs and neutron stars.

nodes.label: H-24 - Micro black holes fail to Hawking-radiate yet still discharge, remaining stable and neutral
nodes.title: Contrary to standard theory, TeV-scale black holes produced in parton collisions do not decay via Hawking radiation, but do rapidly neutralize their color and electric charge through the Schwinger pair-creation mechanism (or some other mechanism), leaving stable, electrically neutral black holes that interact with matter only gravitationally.

nodes.label: H-27 - Under standard gravity, collider collisions are at least 22 orders of magnitude too weak to form a black hole
nodes.title: With ordinary (3+1-dimensional, Einstein) gravitation, no collision at RHIC — or any accelerator in the foreseeable future — comes remotely close to the energy concentration needed for gravitational collapse or singularity formation; conservative dimensionless measures put classical effects at 1e-22 and quantum-gravity effects at 1e-34 of the strength required.

nodes.label: H-28 - Strange quark matter may be absolutely stable in bulk at zero external pressure
nodes.title: Witten's conjecture: quark matter containing roughly equal numbers of up, down and strange quarks may have lower energy per baryon than ordinary nuclear matter even at zero external pressure, making strange matter the true ground state of baryonic matter, with strange-quark nuggets possibly stable for almost any baryon number A between ordinary nuclei (A <~ 263) and neutron stars (A ~ 1e57) - not excluded by the stability of ordinary nuclei, since decay of a nucleus to a strangelet would require many simultaneous weak interactions.

nodes.label: H-29 - A metastable negatively charged strangelet stopped in matter would grow exponentially by alternating nuclear absorption and electron capture
nodes.title: If a strangelet that is stable (or metastable with lifetime ≳ 1e-7 s) and negatively charged in bulk were produced at a collider slowly enough to stop, it would be captured by a nucleus, absorb it exothermically, restore negative charge by electron capture, and repeat — its baryon number growing exponentially with time, since participating quarks grow linearly with A — until all available material had been converted to strange matter; there is no known absolute barrier to this runaway.

nodes.label: H-3 - A TeV string scale would make LHC collisions produce strong-gravity phenomena including black holes
nodes.title: If the fundamental (higher-dimensional) gravity or string scale lies near 1 TeV - via large extra dimensions or strong warping - then LHC parton collisions can produce strong-gravity phenomena including microscopic black holes of minimum mass roughly 3-5 times the fundamental scale, together with, in the string realization, narrow Regge excitations of SM particles and large-missing-energy graviton-emission events.

nodes.label: H-31 - Our vacuum may be a metastable false vacuum that decays by true-vacuum bubble nucleation
nodes.title: The state we call the vacuum may be only a local minimum of the energy — a false vacuum, stable classically but rendered unstable by quantum barrier penetration, decaying through the materialization of bubbles of true vacuum at a calculable rate per unit time per unit volume; such false vacua occur naturally in unified electroweak and grand unified theories.

nodes.label: H-33 - The short-distance structure of gravity is something not listed here
nodes.title: The answer is something not listed here: the true short-distance completion of gravity is neither ordinary Einstein gravity with a Planckian fundamental scale, nor the large-flat-extra-dimension (ADD / TeV-string) scenario, nor a single warped extra dimension — e.g. a low fundamental scale realized through some mechanism no paper in this corpus states.

nodes.label: H-34 - Low-scale quantum gravity would first show at the LHC as something not listed here
nodes.title: The answer is something not listed here: if the quantum-gravity scale lies within collider reach, the earliest and dominant LHC manifestation would be something other than black-hole production with Regge excitations, spin-two graviton resonances, or enhanced transverse two-body final states.

nodes.label: H-35 - An LHC strong-gravity object would behave in some way not listed here
nodes.title: The answer is something not listed here: the correct description of an LHC-produced strong-gravity object and its decay is neither prompt canonical Hawking evaporation, nor microcanonically suppressed metastability, nor non-thermal near-threshold stringy states, nor a stable neutral non-radiating hole — some decay behaviour this literature does not propose.

nodes.label: H-36 - A stable trapped black hole would lead to some outcome not listed here
nodes.title: The answer is something not listed here: a hypothetically stable black hole trapped inside the Earth would produce an outcome that is neither Eddington-limited catastrophic output nor macroscopically negligible growth over the Sun's remaining lifetime — e.g. an intermediate accretion regime this literature does not describe, damaging on a long but sub-solar-lifetime timescale.

nodes.label: H-37 - The UHECR spectral structure arises from something not listed here
nodes.title: The answer is something not listed here: the observed structure of the ultra-high-energy cosmic-ray spectrum arises from a cause not named by the members — e.g. a shared instrumental energy-scale artifact, exotic top-down sources, or a propagation effect other than those proposed.

nodes.label: H-38 - Steady accretion does not realize the maximal transonic rate
nodes.title: Physical accretion onto a small trapped mass does not realize the maximal transonic (Bondi-type) rate: the actual rate differs materially — e.g. suppressed or altered by angular momentum, radiation and heating feedback, magnetic fields, or microphysical effects — so the transonic interpolation formula misestimates growth.

nodes.label: H-39 - Bulk strange quark matter is not absolutely stable at zero external pressure
nodes.title: Bulk strange quark matter is not absolutely stable at zero external pressure: the energy per baryon of up-down-strange quark matter exceeds that of ordinary nuclear matter once external pressure is removed, so Witten's conjecture fails and ordinary nuclei remain the true ground state of baryonic matter.

nodes.label: H-4 - A single warped extra dimension (RS1) generates the weak-Planck hierarchy exponentially
nodes.title: Spacetime contains one small extra dimension forming a slice of AdS5 bounded by two 3-branes (SM fields on the visible brane); the exponential warp factor e^(-k rc pi) with krc ~ 50 redshifts all visible-brane mass parameters from near-Planckian inputs to the weak scale, solving the hierarchy problem without a large compactification volume.

nodes.label: H-40 - A stopped negative strangelet would not grow without limit
nodes.title: A stopped negatively charged (meta)stable strangelet would not grow without limit: some barrier — evolution of the bulk charge toward positive values, surface and Coulomb effects, or loss of binding at larger baryon number — halts the absorption of nuclei before any macroscopic conversion of ordinary matter.

nodes.label: H-41 - No astrophysically relevant flux of catalyzing GUT monopoles exists
nodes.title: There is no cosmic flux of proton-decay-catalyzing GUT monopoles at astrophysically relevant levels: magnetic monopoles either do not exist, are far rarer than the Parker bound, or do not catalyze nucleon decay with strong-interaction-scale cross sections.

nodes.label: H-42 - Our electroweak vacuum is exactly stable with no decay channel
nodes.title: Our electroweak vacuum is not a decaying false vacuum: it is the true minimum of the potential (or its decay rate is exactly zero), so no true-vacuum bubble-nucleation channel exists.

nodes.label: H-43 - The near-criticality of the Higgs potential is an unexplained coincidence
nodes.title: The near-criticality of the Higgs-potential parameters is an accident: no underlying dynamical or statistical mechanism places the mass parameter and the quartic coupling near the phase boundaries — the proximity is a coincidence of otherwise unrelated parameter values.

nodes.label: H-44 - The cold supranuclear equation of state is softened by exotic degrees of freedom
nodes.title: The equation of state of cold matter at supranuclear densities is soft: exotic degrees of freedom — hyperons, boson condensates, or deconfined quark matter — appear near a few times nuclear saturation density and soften the equation of state below what is needed to support the most massive observed neutron stars.

nodes.label: H-45 - General relativity fails in the strong-field regime of massive neutron stars
nodes.title: General relativity does not correctly describe gravity in the strong-field regime: significant deviations — e.g. dipolar gravitational radiation or spontaneous scalarization from long-range extra fields — appear at the gravitational binding energies of massive neutron stars.

nodes.label: H-46 - The published LHC safety arguments are robust and their conditional bounds carry through
nodes.title: The published LHC safety arguments are robust as arguments: the probability that they are critically flawed is small enough that their stated conditional risk bounds carry through, essentially undiluted, as the actionable all-things-considered catastrophe probability.

nodes.label: H-5 - RS1 predicts TeV-mass spin-2 graviton resonances with weak-scale couplings at colliders
nodes.title: If RS1 is realized, colliders would see a small number of spin-2 Kaluza-Klein graviton excitations with ~TeV masses and couplings of order Energy/TeV (not Energy/M_Pl), each individually detectable and reconstructable from its decay products; at energies somewhat above the weak scale the excited gravitons become strongly coupled and string/M-theory excitations open up from the 4D observer's viewpoint.

nodes.label: H-6 - The near-criticality of the Higgs potential parameters reflects an underlying dynamical or statistical principle
nodes.title: Both parameters of the Higgs potential (m^2 and lambda) lie near critical boundaries separating the electroweak phase from inhospitable phases; this quasi-criticality is not a numerical accident but the signature of an underlying mechanism - e.g. a multiverse whose parameter distribution is dynamically peaked near critical lines (self-organized criticality), or transplanckian dynamics driving lambda(M_Pl) toward zero.

nodes.label: H-7 - Micro black holes follow the microcanonical Casadio-Harms Hawking law and can be metastable
nodes.title: Collider-produced micro black holes in an RS2 warped geometry are quantum objects described by the microcanonical Casadio-Harms treatment - for M_BH < M_N the Hawking luminosity is P5 = M_BH hbar c^6/(15360 pi G^2 M_N^3), strongly suppressed relative to the canonical 4D law - so micro black holes can be quasistable (lifetimes from milliseconds up to years) for experimentally allowed values of the warping length L, without ad-hoc assumptions.

nodes.label: H-8 - An LHC-produced metastable black hole could accrete at the Eddington limit and become catastrophic
nodes.title: For un-excluded parameter choices in the microcanonical scenario (illustration - L = 10^-7 m, M_N = 1.9x10^5 kg), an LHC-produced micro black hole captured by Earth grows by subatomic then Bondi accretion within milliseconds to ~1 kg, then sits in Eddington-limited steady state radiating Hawking power ~5x10^16 W (~1300x Earth's geothermal flux, 12 Mt TNT per second) for ~3x10^17 years - threatening catastrophic global heating and earthquakes, or, if accreting near the surface, a locally contained nuclear-explosion-scale accident at CERN.

nodes.label: H-9 - The UHECR ankle and suppression are caused by GZK interactions of extragalactic protons with the CMB
nodes.title: The dip at ~4.6e18 eV (e+e- pair production) and the flux suppression at ~5.4e19 eV are caused by interactions of extragalactic protons with cosmic-microwave-background photons (the GZK process), implying a predominantly protonic composition at the highest energies - rather than the suppression being an instrumental artifact or an intrinsic cutoff of the sources.

nodes.label: H-17 - Any microscopic black hole produced at the LHC decays essentially instantly rather than persisting
nodes.title: If LHC collisions produce microscopic black holes at all (possible only in TeV-scale-gravity scenarios), those black holes decay — via Hawking radiation and/or the quantum-mechanical link between production and decay — on timescales so short that they disintegrate before reaching the detector walls; they are not stable objects.

nodes.label: H-18 - Black holes emit thermal radiation at temperature kappa over 2 pi and eventually evaporate
nodes.title: Quantum effects cause a black hole to create and emit particles exactly like a hot body of temperature kappa/2pi (about 10^26 M^-1 K for mass M in grams, kappa the surface gravity); the emission drains the hole's mass, and absent other effects a hole radiates away all its mass in of order 10^-28 M^3 seconds (M in grams), disappearing entirely.

nodes.label: H-19 - A primordial black hole has decayed away within the age of the universe iff its initial mass was below about 5e14 g
nodes.title: Combining the computed massless-particle emission rates with an estimate for massive-particle emission from smaller holes, a black hole will have fully evaporated within the present age of the universe if and only if its initial mass was M < (5 +/- 1) x 10^14 g — i.e. the evaporation lifetime scales as M^3 with a numerically determined coefficient.

nodes.label: H-23 - TeV-scale gravity with extra dimensions is realized, allowing black-hole production at the LHC
nodes.title: The true (higher-dimensional) Planck scale M_D lies near 1 TeV — via large extra dimensions or strong warping, with characteristic radii R_D from ~5 x 10^-2 cm (D=6) down to ~5 x 10^-13 cm (D=11) for M_D = 1 TeV — so parton collisions at LHC energies can form microscopic black holes of minimum mass roughly 3-5 M_D.

nodes.label: H-25 - The observed suppression at 6e19 eV is the GZK cutoff from photo-pion losses on the CMB
nodes.title: The spectral break HiRes observes at ~6×10^19 eV is the Greisen-Zatsepin-Kuzmin cutoff - extragalactic protons losing energy by photo-pion production on cosmic-microwave-background photons via the Δ+ resonance, limiting their range to ~50 Mpc - rather than a source-spectrum feature; correspondingly, the earlier AGASA unbroken-spectrum result was wrong.

nodes.label: H-26 - Black holes emit a real thermal Hawking flux independent of collapse details
nodes.title: A black hole - whether formed by collapse or preexistent with appropriate boundary conditions on the past horizon - emits a physically real thermal flux of particles as seen by distant observers (Hawking evaporation), the prediction being independent of the details of the collapse that formed it.

nodes.label: H-30 - The hierarchy problem is solved by n of 2 or more large gravity-only extra dimensions with the true Planck scale at about a TeV
nodes.title: There exist n ≥ 2 compact extra spatial dimensions, large compared to the weak scale (up to ~1 mm for n=2), in which gravity but not the Standard Model propagates (SM fields being confined to a 4D submanifold of weak-scale thickness); the fundamental (4+n)-dimensional Planck scale is ~m_EW ~ 1 TeV — the only short-distance scale in nature — and the apparent 4D Planck scale of 1e18-1e19 GeV is just the dilution of gravity in the extra-dimensional volume. Consequences: gravity becomes comparable to gauge forces at ~TeV, so the LHC and NLC would probe strong quantum gravity; for n=2 Newton's law turns over from 1/r^2 to 1/r^4 at 100 microns - 1 mm.

nodes.label: H-32 - Bulk strange quark matter with roughly equal up, down and strange numbers may be stable at almost any baryon number
nodes.title: Nuclear matter consisting of aggregates of up, down and strange quarks in roughly equal proportions may be less massive than ordinary nuclear matter of the same quark number, and such strange-quark nuggets may be stable for almost any baryon number A — including all values intermediate between ordinary nuclei (A ≲ 263) and neutron stars (A ~ 1e57).
