nodes.label: Main question
nodes.title: main report - Was the risk that LHC collisions destroy the Earth (via stable micro black holes or other proposed mechanisms) truly put to rest, and what does that conclusion hinge on

nodes.label: O-1 - PSR J1614-2230 pulsar mass measured at 1.97 solar masses via Shapiro delay
nodes.title: Radio timing of the binary millisecond pulsar PSR J1614-2230 (GBT/GUPPI, March 2010 dense orbital campaign plus 2002-2010 long-term data) detects a very-high-significance Shapiro delay from a nearly edge-on orbit (inclination 89.17 +/- 0.02 deg), yielding a neutron-star mass of 1.97 +/- 0.04 Msun and a white-dwarf companion mass of 0.500 +/- 0.006 Msun; the measurement depends only on general relativity, not on neutron-star models.

nodes.label: O-10 - Telescope Array SD spectrum shows an ankle at 4.6e18 eV and a 5.5-sigma flux suppression at 5.4e19 eV
nodes.title: Four years of Telescope Array surface-detector data (May 2008 - May 2012; 13,100 events above 10^18.2 eV; exposure 3690 km^2 sr yr) yield a UHECR spectrum with a broken-power-law ankle at (4.6 +/- 0.3)e18 eV and a steepening at (5.4 +/- 0.6)e19 eV; extrapolating the pre-break power law predicts 58.6 events above the break versus 21 observed, rejecting an unbroken spectrum at 5.5 sigma, with E_1/2 measured at log10(E/eV) = 19.72 +/- 0.05.

nodes.label: O-11 - TA spectrum agrees with HiRes, and with Auger up to a 20 percent energy-scale shift except a 3-sigma difference in break energy
nodes.title: The TA SD spectrum agrees very well with the HiRes fluorescence spectrum (both above and below 10^18.85 eV), and after allowing a ~20% relative energy-scale difference agrees with the Pierre Auger spectrum except for the suppression energy - (5.4 +/- 0.6)e19 eV (TA) versus (2.9 +/- 0.2)e19 eV (Auger), a 3-standard-deviation difference; once energy scales are normalized, fluorescence and surface-scintillator techniques give consistent spectra, contrary to the earlier AGASA excess.

nodes.label: O-12 - Measured error rates in published scientific arguments and calculations are of order 1e-3 or higher
nodes.title: Empirical estimates of error rates in professional scientific and technical work are substantial: the raw MEDLINE retraction rate is 6.3e-5, with a statistical model putting the true rate at 1e-3 to 1e-2 if all journals received top-tier scrutiny; ~11% of statistical results in a random sample of Nature and BMJ papers were flawed; hospital drug-dose calculation errors run ~1-2% of administrations; audits of real-world spreadsheets find error rates ~88%.

nodes.label: O-13 - Castle Bravo (1954) yielded 15 Mt against a predicted 4-8 Mt because the model neglected lithium-7 reactions
nodes.title: The 1954 Castle Bravo nuclear test yielded 15 megatons against a predicted 4-8 megatons because the Los Alamos model of the explosion neglected the contribution of lithium-7 reactions, which supplied the bulk of the energy - a model failure inside a carefully checked, high-stakes hard-science calculation.

nodes.label: O-14 - The RHIC strangelet safety analysis ran five years before its anthropic-selection flaw was found and corrected
nodes.title: The cosmic-ray-based strangelet safety analysis (Dar, De Rújula et al. 1999) that was pivotal to the RHIC safety report contained an unnoticed flaw - it ignored anthropic observation-selection effects, so Earth's survival could not carry the evidential weight assigned to it - and the gap was only identified and corrected by Tegmark & Bostrom in 2005, after RHIC had operated for about five years.

nodes.label: O-15 - Auger combined spectrum from 310,000 events - ankle 5.1 EeV, instep 13 EeV above 5.5 sigma, suppression 48 EeV
nodes.title: The Pierre Auger Observatory's combined vertical+inclined surface-detector spectrum (2004-2022; ~310,000 events above 2.5 EeV; exposure 104,900 +/- 3,100 km^2 sr yr, the largest UHECR exposure to date) establishes four spectral features - ankle at E12 = 5.1 +/- 0.1 (stat) +/- 1.1 (syst) EeV, instep at E23 = 13 +/- 1 +/- 2 EeV (significance now above 5.5 sigma), suppression at E34 = 48 +/- 2 +/- 5 EeV with post-suppression index gamma4 = 5.4 +/- 0.3 +/- 1.1 - and records zero events above 10^2.25 EeV (~1.8e20 eV) with 90%-CL flux upper limits of ~5e-7 EeV^-1 km^-2 sr^-1 yr^-1 there.

nodes.label: O-16 - Auger energy spectra are declination-independent from -90 to +44.8 degrees apart from the known dipolar modulation
nodes.title: Energy spectra measured in five declination bands spanning -90 to +44.8 degrees are statistically consistent with the full-sky spectrum (departure probabilities 13-71%), with no significant declination dependence beyond the mild modulation expected from the previously measured dipolar anisotropy; the northernmost band (+24.8 to +44.8 deg, inclined events only) also matches, and the spectral parameters there show no deviation from Telescope Array's ~20%-larger-exposure measurements of the same sky.

nodes.label: O-18 - PSR J0348+0432 pulsar mass measured at 2.01 solar masses via spectroscopy plus radio timing
nodes.title: Phase-resolved VLT spectroscopy of the white-dwarf companion (K_WD = 351 +/- 4 km/s; Teff = 10120 +/- 47 +/- 90 K; log g = 6.035 +/- 0.032 +/- 0.060) combined with radio timing (K_PSR = 30.008235 km/s) gives mass ratio q = 11.70 +/- 0.13 and, with white-dwarf model atmospheres/cooling tracks giving M_WD = 0.165-0.185 Msun (99.73% CL), a pulsar mass of 2.01 +/- 0.04 Msun for PSR J0348+0432 - a 39-ms pulsar in a compact 2.46-hour orbit, measured independently of Shapiro delay, in a system with a white-dwarf cooling age of ~2 Gyr.

nodes.label: O-19 - PSR J0348+0432 orbital decay matches the general-relativistic gravitational-wave prediction
nodes.title: Combined Arecibo, Effelsberg, and GBT timing of PSR J0348+0432 measures a significant orbital decay Pb-dot = (-2.73 +/- 0.45)e-13 s/s, which after negligible kinematic corrections ((0.016 +/- 0.003)e-13 s/s) is entirely consistent with the general-relativity prediction for gravitational-wave emission: Pb-dot(obs)/Pb-dot(GR) = 1.05 +/- 0.18 - a strong-field regime (a ~2-Msun neutron star with ~2x the fractional gravitational binding energy of the double-pulsar neutron stars) untested before.

nodes.label: O-2 - PSR J1614-2230 is an old evolved system with characteristic age 5.2 Gyr
nodes.title: PSR J1614-2230 is a fully recycled millisecond pulsar (P = 3.15 ms) with an evolved helium-carbon-oxygen white-dwarf companion and a spin-down characteristic age of 5.2 Gyr, so this ~2-Msun neutron star - with inferred central baryon density ~2-5 times nuclear saturation and radius 11-15 km (only 2-3 Schwarzschild radii) - has persisted at supranuclear density for Gyr timescales.

nodes.label: O-20 - LHC parton luminosities fall steeply with invariant mass, concentrating any threshold process just above threshold
nodes.title: Measured proton parton distribution functions imply LHC parton luminosities that fall steeply with parton center-of-mass energy - roughly like 1/ŝ for qq̄ near 1-2 TeV and approaching 1/ŝ⁴ at higher invariant masses - so production of any above-threshold object is overwhelmingly dominated by events just above its threshold.

nodes.label: O-22 - Earth, Sun, white dwarfs, neutron stars and the stars at large have survived billions of years of cosmic-ray bombardment
nodes.title: Astronomical bodies exposed to cosmic-ray bombardment for billions of years — the Earth (4.5 Gyr), the Sun, and the ~1e22 observable stars including white dwarfs and neutron stars with Gyr-scale observed lifetimes — continue to exist with no sign of any large-scale consequence, and conventional astrophysics accounts for all detected astrophysical black holes.

nodes.label: O-23 - The Moon, unshielded by any atmosphere, has endured billions of years of cosmic-ray heavy-ion bombardment and remains ordinary matter
nodes.title: The Moon has no atmosphere, so cosmic rays — which include a significant heavy-ion (notably iron) component — have struck iron-rich lunar surface material directly for billions of years, producing heavy-ion collisions at energies comparable to or exceeding man-made experiments; the Moon persists as ordinary matter.

nodes.label: O-26 - STAR found no strangelets - upper limit 1e-6 per central Au-Au collision for lifetime above 0.1 ns and mass above 30 GeV
nodes.title: The STAR experiment at RHIC searched for strangelets and found none, setting an upper limit below 1e-6 strangelets per central Au+Au collision for strangelets with lifetimes longer than 0.1 ns and mass larger than 30 GeV.

nodes.label: O-27 - Super-Kamiokande finds no solar-direction excess of 19-55 MeV neutrinos in 2853 live days
nodes.title: In 2853 live days of Super-Kamiokande data (SK-I/II/III, 22.5 kton fiducial volume), a search for low-energy neutrinos from the Sun's direction in the 19-55 MeV electron total-energy range found 317 final candidates with no significant solar-correlated excess: the fit gives a monopole-induced neutrino flux I0 = 102 +/- 64 cm^-2 s^-1 (a 1.6 sigma effect; chi-square(I0=0) = 20.02 for 19 dof, probability 39.3%), yielding a 90% CL upper limit I90 = 166.6 cm^-2 s^-1 on the total flux of all neutrino flavors.

nodes.label: O-28 - Super-K bound on GUT-monopole flux times catalysis cross section from solar non-observation
nodes.title: Under the Rubakov-Callan solar-catalysis model, the Super-K null result translates into a 90% CL monopole flux bound F_M (sigma_0/1 mb) < 6.3 x 10^-24 (beta_M/10^-3)^2 cm^-2 s^-1 sr^-1 for monopole velocities beta_M < 10^-3, valid for monopole masses up to ~10^17 GeV — more than eight orders of magnitude below the best direct cosmic-ray flux limits and two orders below the earlier Kamiokande catalysis search.

nodes.label: O-3 - As of 1998 no laboratory or astrophysical constraint excluded a TeV type-I string scale
nodes.title: As of 1998, no laboratory or astrophysical measurement excluded the TeV-string large-extra-dimension scenario; the strongest accelerator constraint - four-electron contact operators, accurately tested only up to ~100 GeV - is satisfied provided the string scale M_I is above ~1 TeV (and photon Regge excitations above ~300 GeV).

nodes.label: O-30 - The Fly's Eye stereo spectrum shows a dip centered at 10^18.5 eV and a flattening above 10^19 eV
nodes.title: The stereo Fly's Eye spectrum steepens after 10^17.6 eV (index -3.27 +/- 0.02 over 10^17.6-10^18.5 eV) and flattens after 10^18.5 eV (index -2.71 +/- 0.10 over 10^18.5-10^19.6 eV), forming a dip centered at 10^18.5 eV: 5477 events observed between 10^17.6 and 10^19.6 eV against 5936.3 expected from the overall fit, a 5.96 sigma deficit; above 10^18.5 eV, 281 events against 230 expected (3.4 sigma excess). The monocular data independently show a flattening above 10^19 eV (297 events vs 242.35 expected, 3.5 sigma; chance probability ~2 x 10^-4).

nodes.label: O-31 - Fly's Eye detected a 3 x 10^20 eV air shower on 1991 October 15
nodes.title: On 1991 October 15 the monocular Fly's Eye observed a well-measured shower of energy (3.0 +0.36/-0.54) x 10^20 eV — far above the GZK cutoff energy of ~5 x 10^19 eV — with depth of maximum X_max = 852 +68/-100 g/cm^2, consistent with a normal proton-induced shower (proton expectation 850 g/cm^2); a gamma-ray primary cannot be ruled out.

nodes.label: O-33 - Massive white dwarfs with very low magnetic fields and ages from 100 Myr to gigayears are observed
nodes.title: Spectropolarimetric surveys identify white dwarfs combining mass M >~ 1 solar mass, polar magnetic field B_p <~ few x 10^5 G, and cooling ages T >~ 100 Myr — e.g. WD0346-011 (1.25 Msun, B_p < 1.2 x 10^5 G, ~100 Myr), WD1022-301 (1.2 Msun, < 1.2 x 10^5 G, >~100 Myr), WD2159-754 (1.17 Msun, < 3 x 10^4 G, ~2.5 Gyr), WD1236-495 (1.1 Msun, < 3 x 10^4 G, >~1 Gyr), WD2359-434 (0.98 Msun, B_l ~ 3 x 10^3 G, ~1.5 Gyr); masses from spectroscopic surface gravities cross-checked by parallax and gravitational redshift, ages from cooling models, fields down to kG level by Zeeman spectropolarimetry.

nodes.label: O-34 - Neutron stars with gigayear lifetimes, fields of 10^8 G and up, and long-lived binary companions are observed
nodes.title: Neutron stars are observed as robust long-lived objects, with lifetimes significantly exceeding 10^9 yr (including billion-year-old X-ray binary systems), and measured surface magnetic fields ranging upward from ~10^8 G (lowest measured ~100 MG); known X-ray-binary classes (low-mass, massive, red-giant/millisecond-pulsar, ultracompact He-WD donors) give companion solid-angle exposures of 2-30 Myr full-coverage-equivalent over ~Gyr mass-transfer lifetimes, with 2-6 Myr very confident.

nodes.label: O-35 - Earth and Sun have existed for about 4.5 billion years and the Sun has about 5 billion years remaining
nodes.title: The Earth and the Sun have existed, continuously and without destruction, for ~4.5 billion years, and the Sun's natural remaining lifetime — the horizon beyond which Earth is sterilized regardless of any black-hole scenario — is about 5 billion years.

nodes.label: O-36 - HiRes monocular spectra show a flux suppression at 5.6e19 eV with 5.3-sigma significance
nodes.title: HiRes monocular air-fluorescence spectra (HiRes-I May 1997-Jun 2005, HiRes-II Dec 1999-Aug 2004; cumulative exposure above the GZK threshold more than twice AGASA's) show a break at log10 E = 19.75 ± 0.04 - measured cutoff energy (5.6 ± 0.5(stat) ± 0.9(syst)) × 10^19 eV - with 13 events observed above the break against 43.2 expected for an unbroken spectrum (Poisson probability 7e-8, 5.3 sigma), plus the ankle at log10 E = 18.65 ± 0.05; spectral indices 3.25 ± 0.01 (below ankle), 2.81 ± 0.03 (ankle to cutoff), 5.1 ± 0.7 (above cutoff).

nodes.label: O-37 - HiRes E-half energy of 10^19.73 eV matches the theoretical GZK prediction of 10^19.76 eV
nodes.title: The energy E1/2 at which the integral HiRes spectrum falls to half of the no-cutoff expectation is measured as 10^(19.73 ± 0.07) eV, in excellent agreement with the Berezinsky-Grigor'eva robust theoretical GZK value of 10^19.76 eV.

nodes.label: O-39 - Cosmic-ray data imply 1e28 iron-iron collisions at AGS-equivalent energies on the Moon and 1e47 in our past light cone
nodes.title: Folding the measured cosmic-ray fluxes and abundances with geometry yields: iron nuclei above 10 GeV/nucleon collide with iron on the lunar surface ~6e10 times per second, ~1e28 times over 5 Gyr (~8e29 for all targets scaled per eq. 15); interstellar Fe-Fe collisions with all CM-velocity components below 0.1c occur at ~1e-45 cm-3 s-1 at 10 GeV/nucleon (eq. 7); and our past light cone contains ~1e47 Fe-Fe collisions above 100 GeV/nucleon CM-equivalent (eq. 8, updating Hut-Rees) — versus ~2e11 Au-Au collisions in RHIC's planned lifetime.

nodes.label: O-4 - NNLO absolute stability of the SM vacuum up to the Planck scale requires Mh above 129.4 GeV, excluded at 98 percent CL by the measured Higgs mass
nodes.title: The first complete NNLO analysis of the SM Higgs potential gives the absolute-stability condition Mh > 129.4 + 1.4[(Mt-173.1 GeV)/0.7] - 0.5[(as(MZ)-0.1184)/0.0007] +- 1.0_th GeV, i.e. Mh > 129.4 +- 1.8 GeV combined; with the measured Mh = 125.5 +- 0.5 GeV, absolute stability of the SM potential up to M_Pl is excluded at 98 percent CL (one-sided) for Mh < 126 GeV. The new two-loop threshold corrections to lambda reduce the theory error on the critical Mh from ~3 GeV to ~1 GeV.

nodes.label: O-40 - No vacuum-decay transition has occurred anywhere in our past light cone
nodes.title: We observe a universe in which no transition to a lower vacuum state has been triggered anywhere in our past light cone: such a transition would have propagated outward at the speed of light and destroyed the conditions for our existence, and we also know our world already sits in the correct, stable vacuum of QCD.

nodes.label: O-42 - Fifteen years of searches found no stable strange matter - terrestrial isotope, astrophysical, and AGS-SPS accelerator searches all null
nodes.title: Despite vigorous searching there is no evidence for stable strange matter anywhere: searches for anomalously heavy isotopes of normal elements in terrestrial matter set extremely strong limits; no sub-millisecond pulsar or other astrophysical signature of quark stars has been found; and accelerator strangelet searches at the AGS (Au at 11.5 GeV/nucleon) and CERN SPS (Pb at 158, S at 200 GeV/nucleon) on heavy targets, sensitive to both charge signs, |Z/A| ≲ 0.3, masses 5-100 GeV, lifetimes > 50 ns, all found nothing — limits of order one strangelet per 1e7-1e9 collisions.

nodes.label: O-43 - As of 1998 gravity had been accurately measured only down to about a centimeter - 33 untested orders of magnitude above the Planck length
nodes.title: While electroweak interactions were probed at distances of ~1e-16 cm, gravity had (as of 1998) been accurately measured only down to the ~1 cm range; treating the Planck mass ~1e18 GeV as a fundamental scale therefore rests on extrapolating unmodified Newtonian gravity across ~33 orders of magnitude in distance below anywhere it had been tested.

nodes.label: O-5 - With measured Higgs and top masses the SM electroweak vacuum is metastable with lifetime vastly exceeding the age of the universe
nodes.title: Extrapolating the pure SM (no new physics below M_Pl) with the measured Higgs and top masses, the effective potential turns negative at an instability scale of 10^(11+-1) GeV, but quantum tunneling out of the electroweak vacuum is slow enough that the SM parameters lie well inside the meta-stability region of the phase diagram - the expected vacuum lifetime enormously exceeds the age of the universe.

nodes.label: O-6 - The Higgs quartic coupling and its beta function both nearly vanish at the Planck scale
nodes.title: The best-fit Higgs quartic coupling at the Planck scale is small and negative - lambda(M_Pl) = -0.0129 + 0.0028(Mh - 125.5 GeV) +- 0.0047_Mt +- 0.0018_as +- 0.0028_th - with exact vanishing excluded at 2 sigma; its beta function vanishes at ~3x10^17 GeV, so the measured SM parameters put the Higgs potential remarkably close to the critical boundary between the stability and metastability phases.

nodes.label: O-7 - Torsion-balance experiments limit the RS2 warping length L to below 44 microns
nodes.title: Sub-millimeter torsion-balance tests of Newtonian gravity (Kapner et al. 2007) bound the RS2 warped-dimension curvature length to L < 44 microns at 95 percent CL; values of L up to 44 microns - in particular the 15.3-44 micron range - remain experimentally allowed.

nodes.label: O-41 - The Moon persists as ordinary matter after 5 billion years of unshielded cosmic-ray bombardment
nodes.title: The Moon — atmosphere-free, its surface rich in iron, its composition known directly from Apollo samples — has been struck by cosmic rays including heavy nuclei for ~5 Gyr and remains ordinary matter; it has not been converted to strange matter.

nodes.label: O-17 - Human observers exist today, so every historical record is conditioned on no observer-extinguishing catastrophe having occurred
nodes.title: Intelligent observers (humanity) exist on Earth at the present epoch; consequently the evolutionary chain leading to us was never broken by a terminally catastrophic event, and any historical or geological record we compile is necessarily sampled only from histories compatible with our existence.

nodes.label: O-21 - Cosmic rays above 1e17 eV strike Earth at 5e-14 per cm2 per s - 3e22 above-LHC-energy collisions over Earth's history and 1e31 universe-wide
nodes.title: The measured cosmic-ray spectrum extends to ~1e20 eV, and the total flux at or above 1e17 eV — the fixed-target energy whose centre-of-mass energy equals the LHC's 14 TeV — is about 5e-14 per cm2 per second; over Earth's 5e18 cm2 surface and 4.5 Gyr age this gives over 3e22 above-LHC-energy collisions, i.e. ~1e5 full LHC programmes on Earth, ~1e9 on the Sun, ~1e31 across the observable universe's stars since its origin, and ~3e13 complete LHC programmes per second ongoing.

nodes.label: O-24 - RHIC particle abundances including multi-strange baryons match a thermal model at T about 165 MeV with baryon chemical potential falling as collision energy rises
nodes.title: Relative abundances of all measured particle species in Au+Au collisions at RHIC (sqrt(s_NN) = 130 and 200 GeV) — including particles with one, two or three strange quarks down to per-mille-level Omega baryons — are well described by a statistical hadron-gas model with chemical freeze-out temperature saturating at T ≈ 165 MeV (~1.6e12 K) and a baryon chemical potential mu_B that decreases as collision energy increases; measured light-nuclei penalty factors (1/50 at AGS to ~1/300 at SPS; antinuclei 1/(2e5) AGS to 1/1500 RHIC) follow the same thermal systematics, Cu+Cu collisions are described by the same model, and observed velocity distributions are similar to or broader than those assumed in the earlier RHIC safety study.

nodes.label: O-25 - RHIC fireballs expand explosively and die within 1e-23 s with no strange-antistrange asymmetry - the conditions strangeness distillation needs are absent
nodes.title: RHIC experiments find the net nucleon (baryon) density of the produced matter is small and decreases at higher collision energies, the plasma expands rapidly at about half the speed of light and falls apart within ~1e-23 s (~10 fm/c), collective-flow gradients increase with energy (explosive, bulk emission rather than slow surface evaporation), and no characteristic difference is observed between the production of particles containing strange quarks and strange antiquarks.

nodes.label: O-29 - Fly's Eye cosmic-ray differential energy spectrum above 10^17 eV follows a power law of index about -3.07
nodes.title: The monocular Fly's Eye (Dugway, Utah; 1981 through July 1992) measures a cosmic-ray differential energy spectrum J(E) = 10^-29.55 x (E/10^18 eV)^-3.07 +/- 0.01 m^-2 s^-1 sr^-1 eV^-1 over 10^17.3 - 10^19.9 eV; the higher-resolution stereo data give an overall slope of -3.18 +/- 0.02 over 10^17.3 - 10^19.6 eV.

nodes.label: O-32 - Cosmic rays have produced of order 10^22 nucleon-nucleon collisions above LHC energy on Earth alone
nodes.title: Cosmic rays strike Earth with energies observed to exceed 10^20 eV (nucleon-nucleon CM energies above 100 TeV); a lower-bound flux relation dPhi/dE ~ 10^6 (E/GeV)^-3 m^-2 s^-1 sr^-1 GeV^-1 for E > 10^17 eV integrates, for primaries of mass number A, to N(sqrt(s) > 14 TeV) ~ (1.6 x 10^3)/A yr^-1 km^-2 sr^-1 — about 10^22/A nucleon-nucleon collisions above LHC CM energy over Earth's existence, vastly exceeding the ~10^17 collisions of the LHC's lifetime (10^9 s^-1 over ~10^8 s).

nodes.label: O-38 - Measured cosmic-ray fluxes follow E^-2.5 to E^-2.7 power laws with solar-system composition - iron measured up to 2 TeV per nucleon
nodes.title: Cosmic-ray fluxes are measured up to total energies of order 1e20 eV; every measured species follows a power law dF/dE ∝ E^-gamma with gamma ≈ 2.5-2.7, relative nuclear abundances track solar-system abundances to within about an order of magnitude at all measured energies, iron flux is measured up to ~2 TeV/nucleon with Gamma(Fe, 10 GeV) ≈ 4e-3 per steradian-second-m2-GeV, and nuclei with Z > 70 ('gold') have abundance ~1e-5 relative to iron.

nodes.label: O-8 - MoEDAL found no trapped magnetic charge at or above one Dirac charge in 794 kg of aluminum exposed to 13 TeV LHC collisions
nodes.title: All 2400 aluminum samples of the full MoEDAL trapping detector (794 kg, forward + lateral regions at the LHCb interaction point), exposed to 4.0 fb^-1 of 13 TeV pp collisions in 2015-2017, were scanned at least twice with a dc SQUID magnetometer; no sample contained a persistent magnetic charge >= 1 g_D. All 87 above-threshold (>0.4 g_D) candidate samples resolved to instrumental artifacts on repeated scans; the estimated false-negative probability at 1 g_D is below 0.2 percent.

nodes.label: O-9 - MoEDAL sets 95 percent CL cross-section limits down to 11 fb and indicative mass limits of 1500-3750 GeV for LHC monopole pair production
nodes.title: From the trapping null result, 95 percent CL upper limits on the monopole pair-production cross section in 13 TeV pp collisions reach down to 11 fb (improving MoEDAL's previous 40 fb), interpreted for Drell-Yan and - for the first time at the LHC - photon-fusion production, for spins 0, 1-2 and 1, both beta-independent and beta-dependent couplings, and magnetic charges 1-5 g_D; comparison with leading-order cross sections yields indicative mass exclusions of ~1500-3750 GeV, the strongest collider monopole limits for charges two to five times the Dirac charge.
